name: auth
context: Create and manage Resend API keys with permission scoping and domain restrictions
info: Resend authenticates all API requests via Bearer tokens. API keys are created in the Resend dashboard or via the API itself. Each key starts with the `re_` prefix and supports two permission levels: Full Access (all CRUD operations on any resource) and Sending Access (email sending only, optionally restricted to a specific domain). Keys should be stored in the `RESEND_API_KEY` environment variable and never committed to source control. This context node teaches agents how to create, rotate, and scope API keys for secure email operations. See [[resend]] for the ecosystem overview and [[resend/send]] for using authenticated keys to send email.
triggers: restrict resend api key to domain
body:
# Resend Authentication Every Resend API call requires an API key passed as a Bearer token in the `Authorization` header. ## API Key Format ``` re_xxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx ``` All Resend API keys start with `re_`. ## Authorization Header ```bash curl -X POST https://api.resend.com/emails \ -H 'Authorization: Bearer re_xxxxxxxxxxxx' \ -H 'Content-Type: application/json' \ -d '{"from":"you@yourdomain.com","to":["user@example.com"],"subject":"Hello","html":"

Hi

"}' ``` ## SDK Initialization ### Node.js ```typescript import { Resend } from 'resend'; const resend = new Resend(process.env.RESEND_API_KEY); ``` ### Python ```python import os import resend resend.api_key = os.environ["RESEND_API_KEY"] ``` ## Creating API Keys Create keys in the [Resend dashboard](https://resend.com/api-keys) or via the API: ```bash curl -X POST https://api.resend.com/api-keys \ -H 'Authorization: Bearer re_xxxxxxxxxxxx' \ -H 'Content-Type: application/json' \ -d '{"name": "production-key", "permission": "full_access"}' ``` Response: ```json { "id": "dacf4072-4119-4d88-932f-6202748ac7c8", "token": "re_c1tpEyD8_NKFusih9vKVQknRAQfmFcWCv" } ``` ## Permission Levels | Permission | Scope | Use Case | |------------|-------|-----------| | `full_access` | Create, delete, get, update any resource | Admin, CI/CD pipelines | | `sending_access` | Send emails only | Application servers, edge functions | ## Domain-Restricted Keys When using `sending_access`, you can restrict a key to a specific domain: ```json { "name": "marketing-key", "permission": "sending_access", "domain_id": "d91cd9bd-1176-453e-8fc1-35364d380206" } ``` This ensures the key can only send from that verified domain — useful for multi-tenant setups or isolating production from staging. ## API Key Management ```bash # List all keys curl https://api.resend.com/api-keys \ -H 'Authorization: Bearer re_xxxxxxxxxxxx' # Delete a key curl -X DELETE https://api.resend.com/api-keys/dacf4072-4119-4d88-932f-6202748ac7c8 \ -H 'Authorization: Bearer re_xxxxxxxxxxxx' ``` ## Security Best Practices 1. **Never hardcode keys** — use environment variables (`RESEND_API_KEY`) 2. **Use least privilege** — prefer `sending_access` over `full_access` for app servers 3. **Restrict by domain** — scope keys to specific verified domains when possible 4. **Rotate regularly** — create new keys and delete old ones periodically 5. **Separate environments** — use different keys for development, staging, and production ## Environment Variables | Variable | Required | Description | |----------|----------|-------------| | `RESEND_API_KEY` | Yes | API key starting with `re_` |

sections.title: API key format and header
sections.body: Resend API keys always start with the `re_` prefix. Pass them as Bearer tokens: `Authorization: Bearer re_xxxxxxxxxxxx`. The Node.js SDK accepts the key in the constructor: `new Resend(process.env.RESEND_API_KEY)`. The Python SDK uses `resend.api_key = os.environ['RESEND_API_KEY']`.

sections.title: Permission levels
sections.body: Two levels: `full_access` grants CRUD on all resources (domains, API keys, emails, contacts). `sending_access` restricts to email sending only. Use `sending_access` for application code and `full_access` only for admin scripts and CI/CD. When using `sending_access`, optionally pass `domain_id` to restrict sending to a single verified domain.

sections.title: Creating and rotating keys
sections.body: Create keys via `POST /api-keys` with `name` and `permission`. The response includes a `token` field — this is the only time the full key is shown. List keys with `GET /api-keys` and delete with `DELETE /api-keys/:id`. Rotate by creating a new key, updating your environment, then deleting the old key.

links.context: Use authenticated keys to send emails
Domain IDs needed for domain-restricted keys

related.note: Authentication is required before sending any email
Domain IDs are used to restrict sending-only API keys
